title: Matelea suberosa
intro: Matelea suberosa, also known as "Ironwood Vine," is a species of flowering plant in the family Apocynaceae, native to the tropical forests of Central and South America. The plant is valued for its potential health benefits, including anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antispasmodic properties, which may aid in the treatment of various conditions such as pain relief, digestive issues, and respiratory problems. Bioactive constituents of Matelea suberosa include alkaloids, flavonoids, and saponins, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. Traditional herbal preparations made with Matelea suberosa include tinctures, teas, and infusions, often used to support overall well-being and alleviate symptoms associated with various health conditions.
taxonomy: Matelea suberosa is classified within the plant family Apocynaceae, commonly known as the dogbane or oleander family. This family comprises over 5,000 species of flowering plants, including various genera such as Plumeria and Nerium. As a member of the Apocynaceae family, Matelea suberosa is part of the Apocynoideae subfamily, which is primarily comprised of twining or climbing plants. The genus Matelea itself is native to the Americas and consists of approximately 60 species, with Matelea suberosa being one of the species found in Central and South America. The classification of Matelea suberosa highlights its unique characteristics and relationships with other plants within the Apocynaceae family.
